pages.text: and thermal insulation. The density can be reduced by replacing part of the solids by air voids or lightweight aggregates, several ap- proaches exist for this objective. In the method known as no-ﬁnes concrete, the ﬁne aggregate is omitted; whereas in the called light- weight concretes, the normal aggregates are replaced by lightweight to reduce the density. If air voids are introduced into the cement paste to reduce its density, the concrete is know as cellular, aerated, or foamed concrete [25]; various routes are available to suit this pur- pose, one of them requires the PC paste or mortar to be mixed with preformed organic foam. Another option uses chemical products mixed with the PC to generate gas within the mixture. One of such chemicals is metallic aluminium powder (Al powder), which is very reactive in alkaline environments, such as calcium hydroxide or alkaline hydroxides; the aluminium dissolves as aluminate Al 2O/C0 and H2 gas is liberated according to the following reaction [26,27]: 4Al þ OH/C0 þ H2O ! 2Al2O/C0 þ 3=2H2 The bubbles of H2 gas are trapped within the cementitious paste or mortar, expanding and increasing the volume. In order to pre- vent the escape of the gas, the paste must have a proper consis- tency and fast setting times. 1.2. Objectives of the study Despite the advantages of lightweight concrete over standard concrete, the lower mechanical properties of the former are a drawback. However, as previously mentioned, properly formulated geopolymers can offer high compressive strengths, which would allow a reduction in density while maintaining acceptable mechanical properties. Yang et al. [28] studied expanded clay as lightweight aggregates in concretes and mortars, based on BFS activated with Na 2O/C1n SiO2; for bulk densities of 775–2259 kg/m 3 the compressive strengths reported were of 3.3–54 MPa after 28 days. Neuschäffer et al. [29] proposed a wide range of composi- tions using Mk as a binder, quartz as ﬁller, Al powder and organic or inorganic peroxides as foaming agents and curing temperatures of 50–200 /C176C; densities of 350–450 kg/m 3 are indicated but with- out mechanical strength data. Other reports indicated properties of the aerated concretes as bulk density and thermal conductivity, i.e. 270 kg/m 3 and 0.067 W/mK [30], or 100–800 kg/m 3 and P0.037 W/mK depending of the density [31]; however, data on the chemical composition of the raw materials, activation solution and the foaming agent concentration are omitted. Vlc ˇ ek et al. pre- pared lightweight mixtures with Al powder using mixtures slags- BFS–FA [32] and BFS–FA [33], the bulk densities and compressive strength were of 534–1030 kg/m 3 and 1.8–5.1 MPa and 639– 1151 kg/m3 and 1.4–5.1 MPa, respectively; the hydration products in the ﬁrst case were C–S–H and zeolites. Nowadays, activated Mk, FA and BFS can be considered as binders in the manufacture of aer- ated concretes. The use of industrial by products is interesting due to ecological and economical advantages. The objective of this contribution is to investigate the effect of composition, curing temperature and density on the strength, ther- mal conductivity, microstructures and composition of reaction products of aerated concretes based composite geopolymeric bind- ers of Mk–FA using BFS as a lightweight aggregate. No reports were found in the literature considering the combination of such mate- rials, type and form, nor the assemblage of results. 2. Experimental study 2.1. Materials Th

40 ± 3 /C176C. The sample (15 g) were ground in a planetary mill (Rets- ch, PM400/2) for 15 min at 300 rpm; the samples were passed through a mesh (#100) of 150 lm of aperture. The samples were ﬁnally analyzed in a diffractometer (Philips, D-Expert 3040) with radiation Cu K a, over the range 7–60 /C1762h with a measuring time of 3 s. 2.3.4. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) Concrete specimens were prepared by mounting pieces in epoxy resin, once set these were ground with SiC sand paper grade of grain sizes of 80–1200. The specimens were further polished with diamond pastes of 3–0.25 lm, acetone was used as lubricant to avoid the presence of humidity; the samples surfaces were coated with graphite to be observed under the SEM (Philips, XL- 30 ESEM), which was accessorized with energy dispersive spec- troscopy (EDS). 3. Results 3.1. Production of gas hydrogen. Displaced volume vs. % Al powder added A series of tests were carried out to determine the amount of Al powder needed to attain the concrete target densities. A kitazato ﬂask was set containing 10 ml of the activating solution, Al powder was added at 0.5–1.7 wt.% and the solution was stirred magneti- cally at room temperature. Hydrogen production started after about 4 min after the addition of Al powder. The gas was evacuated through a pipe, 50 cm in length and 5 mm in diameter, and passed throughout a water bath; the gas was collected in an inverted bur- ette to measure amount produced. Fig. 2 shows the results of gas volume vs. wt.% Al powder; a correction factor was applied when preparing the concretes since not all the gas is retained by the con- crete. This data was used to attain the mentioned target densities. 3.2. Mechanical properties 3.2.1. Compressive strength Fig. 3 shows the compressive strength vs. time for concretes of various densities without BFS sand aggregate, Table 3 gathers all strength results. The general expected trend was that as the den- sity was reduced, the compressive would drop considerably. For 1200 kg/m 3, the higher curing temperature favored the compres- sive strength in the ﬁrst day, the M7512 showed a strength 11 MPa greater than that of M2012 (/C25 3.5 MPa); however for later times the concrete cured at 20 /C176C developed strengths similar to that cured at 75 /C176C. After 28 days the average strength for M7512 and M2012 was around 12 MPa, which are superior to those values of 4.5–5.5 MPa obtained for foamed PC concrete [36]. A report on PC concrete manufactured with lightweight aggregate of expanded polyestirene and silica fume substitutions, indicated that in order to reach strengths of 12 MPa the density required was within the range of 1500–1760 kg/m 3 [37]. Mortars with lightweight aggre- gates with densities among 1200–1244 kg/m 3 reported values of 6.4–22.1 MPa after 28 days [28], which were slightly higher than aerated concretes of this work data for the concrete F7512 is not shown, the concrete was prepared three times, and in all cases the bubbles formed and then collapsed gradually, the gas was lost and the specimens increased in density. The results of aerated concretes with density of 900 kg/m 3 developed values above 4 MPa, even after 1 day of curing. The typ- ical compressive strength for PC concretes foamed with densities of 800–1000 kg/m 3 is of 1.5–3.0 MPa [36]. Demirbog˘ a et al. [38] investigated expanded perlite aggregates and pumice in compos- ites of PC, silica fume and FA concretes of densities among 845– 928 kg/m 3 and

it was noted that after curing at 20 /C176C the addition of BFS sand (replacement of the geopolymeric paste) was not favourable as the strength was reduced; this could be attributed to the fact that the participation of the BFS in the reactions depends strongly on the mechanical and thermal activations, both activation means were absent for BFS sand under these conditions. From the concretes of 900 kg/m 3 that of F7590B developed the highest strength, reaching 4 MPa after 28 days; this was above PC foamed concretes of 1000 kg/m 3 that reach 2.5–3.0 MPa [36]. The concretes M2090B and M7590B reached and maintained strengths of about 2.5 MPa. It is clear that the substitution of Mk by FA was favourable for strength development for curing at 75 /C176C. The effect of the high temperature is advantageous for the ﬁrst day. The strength of concretes with 600 kg/m 3 were always under 2 MPa, although it was above the typical values for PC concrete of this density (1.0–1.5 MPa) [36] and activated aerated concretes BFS– FA with density of 639 kg/m 3 [33]. The concrete M2060B could not be manufactured even after three repetitions as the bubble col- lapsed; more on this phenomenon is discussed below. 3.2.2. Flexural strength The Fig. 5 shows ﬂexural strength vs. time for the same con- cretes described in Fig. 3; Table 4 summarizes all numerical results. Similar to compressive strength, the ﬂexural strength diminished with the density, as it is more sensitive to the defects induced by the trapped bubbles. For the 1200 kg/m 3 concretes, the increment in the curing temperature resulted in higher strength, after 1 day M7512 reached 2.4 MPa while M2012 only 0.5 MPa; however, it was noted that curing at 20 /C176C was more effective in the long term. The strength for concretes of 900 kg/m 3 remained at about 1.0 MPa. For foamed PC concretes, the strength for 600 kg/m 3 was reported at 0.85 MPa [25]; only the concrete F7560 reached such value during the initial stages and reduced to about 0.5 MPa. The Fig. 6 shows the ﬂexural strength vs. time for the same concretes as in Fig. 4 . For concretes of 1200 kg/m 3 the F7512B developed the highest values. Increased curing temperature was favourable; the average strengths of M2012B and M7512B were about 1.8 and 2.5 MPa, respectively; this proved that the presence of the BFS was favourable. For the concretes of 900 kg/m 3 the Table 3 Compressive strength and standard deviation of aerated concretes. tendencies were similar, the highest strengths were for F7590B, followed by M7590B and then M2090B; the average strength of these concretes was around 50–75% of those concretes without aggregate. For 600 kg/m 3, the M7560B presented signiﬁcant varia- tions at 14 and 28 days. However, it stabilized after 90 and 180 days; average strength for F7560B was /C25 0.4 MPa, which is quite under the typical values of 0.85 MPa of foamed concrete of the same density [25]. 3.3. Thermal conductivity of aerated concrete based geopolymer with Al powder The thermal conductivity of geopolymers based on Mk was re- ported as 0.067 W/mK for a density of 270 kg/m 3 [30] and of 0.16 W/mK (compressive strength 0.8–2.0 MPa) produced for den- sities between 350 and 400 kg/m 3 [31]; such reports did not men- tion the method and conditions used to measure the thermal conductivity. On the other hand, Subaer and van Rieser [41] mea- sured the thermal conductivity of geopolymers synthesised with Mk, using a transient hot wire method, values of 0.55–0.65 W/ mK were reported for densities of 1430–1890 kg/m 3. Values of 0.4–1.2 W/mK were reported for geopolymer synthesised with Mk and Na, NaK or K and concluded that the thermal conductivity is higher in geopolymer of Na compared with geopolymers based K [39]. The thermal conductivity for concretes M2012, M2090, M2060 and paste of Mk cured 20 /C176C for 14 days were obtained.Fig. 7 a shows the thermal conductivity vs. density, the shaded area repre- sents data for the thermal conductivity of PC lightweight aggregate and foamed concretes in oven-dry condition [25]. The density of Mk paste was of 1800 kg/m 3 and the thermal conductivity of 1.63 W/mK. The thermal conductivity measured for aerated con- cretes M2012, M2090 and M2060 were 1.22, 0.62 and 0.49 W/ mK, respectively. Fig. 7 b shows the compressive strength of aer- ated concretes vs. density at 28 days, for comparison purposes, data from other reports were included as the solid line for com- mercial foamed concretes [40] and the dotted line [36]. The strength of the aerated concretes investigated here was higher than the reported by Aldridge [36,40] except for the density of 600 kg/m 3. Although the values of conductivity appeared greater than the typical values previously reported for lightweight and foamed con- cretes, it is necessary to consider two factors: 1. The samples were not analyzed under oven-dry conditions and it is know that the presence of water increases the thermal conduc- tivity [25]. 2. In agreement with Subaer and van Rieser [41] the addition 40% of quartz aggregate increased the thermal conductivity by about 40% of geopolymers synthesised from Mk; in this case, the Mk used contained about 30% of quartz and cristobalite, which might have increased the conductivity. 3.4. X-ray diffraction (XRD) Fig. 8 shows the XRD patterns for powdered aerated concretes M2060 and F7560 for 1–180 days, the results from unreacted Mk and 75%Mk–25%FA were also included. The Mk showed strong peaks of the crystalline phases present of quartz and cristobalite, the composite binders also showed mullite from the FA; both raw materials displayed an amorphous hump at 15–30 /C1762h. Lightweight concretes of activated metakaolin-ﬂy ash binders, with blast furnace slag aggregates R. Arellano Aguilar, O. Burciaga Díaz, J.I. Escalante García * Center for Research and Advanced Studies, Saltillo, Coahuila, CP 25900, Mexico article info Article history: Received 5 June 2009 Received in revised form 11 December 2009 Accepted 16 December 2009 Available online 25 January 2010 Keywords: Geopolymers Strength Metakaolin Fly ash Lightweight concrete Blastfurnace slag abstract This work investigated geopolymeric lightweight concretes based on binders composed of metakaolin with 0% and 25% ﬂy ash, activated with 15.2% of Na 2O using sodium silicate of modulus SiO 2/ Na2O = 1.2. Concretes of densities of 1200, 900 and 600 kg/m 3 were obtained by aeration by adding alu- minium powder, in some formulations lightweight aggregate of blast furnace slag was added at a ratio binder:aggregate 1:1; curing was carried out at 20 and 75 /C176C. The compressive and ﬂexural strength development was monitored for up to 180 days. The strength diminished with the reduction of the den- sity and high temperature curing accelerated strength development. The use of the slag had a positive effect on strength for 1200 kg/m 3 concretes; reducing the amount of binder used. The thermal conductiv- ity diminished from 1.65 to 0.47 W/mK for densities from 1800 to 600 kg/m 3. The microstructures revealed dense cementitious matrices conformed of reaction products and unreacted metakaolin and ﬂy ash. Energy dispersive spectroscopy and X-ray diffraction showed the formation of amorphous silicoa- luminate reaction products. /C2112009 Published by Elsevier Ltd. 1. Introduction Portland cement (PC) is the most used binder in the produc- tion of concrete, in 2008 the worldwide production of PC reached 2.83 billion tons [1]. The production of 1 kg of PC generates /C25 1k g of CO2 [2], and considering that CO 2 is the main cause, after water vapor, of the green house effect [3], it is evident that alternative cementitious materials are needed, in order satisfy mankind requirements of construction materials, with the least contribu- tion to the current phenomenon of global warming and the con- sequent environmental impact. In the decade of the 50, Glukovhsky initiated the development of cementitious materials based on alkali activated silicoalumi- nates, which were denominated ‘‘soil silicates” [4]. Later on, Davidovits developed cementitious materials based on alkali acti- vated metakaolin (Mk), called geopolymers [5], which is currently a nomenclature widely used. The production of Mk requires burn- ing temperatures lower than 750 /C176C, which demands less use of fuels relative to the PC processing at 1450 /C176C, also, there is no decarbonation of raw materials; this results in binders of consider- able lower CO 2 emissions than PC. The production of alkali activated cements allows the use of a wide range of materials (minerals and byproducts) with composi- tions within the systems SiO 2–Al2O3 or SiO 2–Al2O3–CaO, such as Mk [6,7], ﬂy ash (FA, byproduct of power plants) [8–10], blast fur- nace slag (BFS, byproduct of the pig iron production) [11,12] or mixtures of them [13–17]. Many of these materials require only milling and no thermal processing to be used as binders, which re- duces even more their environmental impact. The activation is at- tained with alkaline compounds based on NaOH [7,9,10,14,18,19], combinations of KOH and K 2O/C1n SiO2 [13],N a 2O/C1n SiO2 an

/C15 The XDR patterns revealed the formation of new reaction products; unreacted Al powder was not detected. /C15 The SEM microstructures revealed dense microstructures of geopolymeric pastes. A dense and strong interfacial zone with the BFS sand grains was noted as a result of the BFS par- ticipation in the reactions. to most geopolymers reported in the literature [10,16,42], the dif- fractogram of reacted concretes was dominated by a large amor- phous hump at 20–36 /C1762h, the shifting of the hump relative to the raw materials, indicates the formation of aluminosilicate reaction products different nature. The peaks of cristobalite, quartz and mullite remained intense even after 180 days of curing, which indi- cates that they do not participate in the reactions of geopolymer- ization. The amount of Al powder added was lower than 2 wt.%, and considering its reaction in the alkaline system, it was not pos- sible to detect it. 3.5. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) Fig. 9 shows BSE microstructures from concrete M2060 after 1 and 180 days of age. At low magniﬁcations, pores larger than 1 mm, resulting from the liberation of H 2, can be observed. At high- er magniﬁcations grains rich in Si were identiﬁed by EDS, corre- sponding to quartz (Q) (also detected by XRD); these remained unreacted after the alkaline attack even after 180 days. Particles rich in Al and Si corresponding to unreacted Mk (M) were found for both curing ages; more notably at 1 day. It was clear that after 180 days the matrix was more dense, indicating the progressive reaction of Mk and the precipitation of reactions products (G), a Mk particle located at the center-right in the microstructure at 180 days (250/C2 ) shows gradual reaction by a solid state mechanism. Fig. 10 shows the EDS results of 20 spot analyses randomly ta- ken through the microstructure in the reaction products for M2060 after 1 and 180 days; the results are plotted in a ternary diagram SiO 2–Al2O3–Na2O, normalised to 100 wt.%; others oxides were left out, the average compositions and deviation standard were also in- cluded. It can be seen that the average content of Al 2O3 increased slightly after at 180 days, this is in agreement with a report [14] that indicated that the species dissolved from Mk are [Al(OH) 4]/C0 , [SiO(OH)3]/C0 , and [Si 2O(OH)2]2/C0 ; the former are more readily dis- solved and reactive compared with silicate units, this would lead to Al incorporation in the geopolymeric matrix as time advances. The % Na 2O for 1 and 180 days were 7.1% and 7.0%, respectively; Fig. 7. (a) Thermal conductivity of aerated concretes, paste and concretes of various types [25]. (b) Compressive strength at 28 days; ( /C7) aerated concretes, line solid [40] and dotted [36]. Fig. 8. mixing were added. The resulting concretes were cast and isother- mally set at 20 ± 2 /C176Co r7 5 ± 5/C176C for 24 h in acrylic moulds of 40 /C2 40 /C2 160 mm3. Table 2 shows composition and curing condi- tions of the concretes prepared in a simpliﬁed nomenclature. After demoulding the specimens were individually weighed, wrapped in cling ﬁlm and returned to the isothermal chamber of 20 ± 2 /C176C. The setting at 75 /C176C involved water losses, so slightly higher initial den- sities (1330, 930 or 730 kg/m 3) were considered to attain a ﬁnal densities close to those targeted. 2.3. Preparation of samples for characterisation 2.3.1. Compressive and ﬂexural strength The strength evolution was followed using a hydraulic press (controls, 65-L130). Flexural strength was measured in three points using three bars of 40 /C2 40 /C2 160 mm3. Four cubes of 40 mm were cut from the tested bars and used for compressive strength measurements. The density measurements were taken by measuring and weighing the bars before the mechanical testing; the deviation was generally within ±50 kg/m 3. 2.3.2. Thermal conductivity (TC) The test was carried according to ASTM 177-85 [35], and con- sisted of the measurement of steady-state heat ﬂux through paral- lel plates. When the system was stabilized the heat ﬂux and the difference of temperature were measured and the thermal conduc- tivity was obtained; the results represent the average of three rep- etitions. The specimens were prepared under the same procedure described for the bars, but were cast in moulds of 300 /C2 300 /C2 60 mm 3. The samples were covered with plastic ﬁlm during setting and after demoulding during the curing at 20 /C176C un- til the date of test. 2.3.3. X-ray diffraction (XRD) After mechanical testing, fragments were recollected and stored in acetone during for 48 h, and further dried in a vacuum stove at Fig. 1.
